serving.description: 1 serving of roasted lamb ribs (approximately 3–4 ribs)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of roasted lamb ribs is approximately 250 g, comprising 3 to 4 bone-in ribs with spice crust.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, choice, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 17085)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted lamb rib (separable lean and fat, choice, cooked, roasted) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent bone-free edible portion per 100 g. Spice rub (cumin, chili, salt) contributes negligible macronutrients at typical application rates but elevates sodium; sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical Chinese restaurant spice-marinated preparation. This is the closest defensible approved-source match for roasted lamb ribs.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat dishes with negligible carbohydrate content have a glycemic index of effectively zero (no glycemic response). Per Veritas v2 scoring model, glycemic_index for such foods must be set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category to 'low'. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification principles for zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving = 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving of spice-marinated roasted lamb ribs provides approximately 950 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content with high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted lamb ribs contain essentially zero digestible carbohydrate and have a glycemic index of 1, making them unlikely to raise postprandial blood glucose; however, the high saturated fat content (approximately 22 g per serving) warrants attention for overall metabolic health in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 250 g serving delivers approximately 22 g saturated fat and 243 mg cholesterol, both substantially exceeding recommended daily limits; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume roasted lamb ribs only occasionally and in small portions, preferring leaner cuts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Lamb Ribs (特色烤羊排, Kǎo yáng pái) is a Chinese-style dish of bone-in lamb rib sections marinated in a dry spice rub — typically cumin, chili powder, garlic, and salt — then roasted or charcoal-grilled at high heat until the exterior is caramelised and lightly charred while the interior remains juicy and tender. The dish is strongly associated with Xinjiang and Northern Chinese culinary traditions, where lamb is the dominant protein and cumin is the signature spice. In Singapore, it is served at specialist Northern Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and aromatic with a pronounced cumin-chili character and a rich, fatty mouthfeel from the lamb's natural intramuscular fat. Nutritionally, roasted lamb ribs are a high-protein, high-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content. Per 100 g of edible portion, the dish provides approximately 292 kcal, 24.6 g protein, 21 g total fat (of which 8.8 g is saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of zinc (4.4 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg/100 g), iron (1.9 mg/100 g), and selenium (26 mcg/100 g). The glycemic index is effectively zero (set to 1 per Veritas v2 convention) due to the absence of digestible carbohydrate. Clinically, the dish is low-concern for blood glucose management but moderate-to-high concern for cardiovascular health due to its saturated fat and cholesterol load, and moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from the spice marinade. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified lamb.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chinese roasted lamb ribs?
Is roasted lamb ribs high in fat?
Can diabetics eat roasted lamb ribs?
What is the GI of lamb ribs?
Bei Jiang roasted lamb ribs calories
Is roasted lamb ribs gluten free?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted lamb rib data (NDB No. 17085), which is an approved primary source and the closest defensible match for this preparation. No Singapore-specific or Chinese-restaurant-specific nutrient analysis for roasted lamb ribs was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Sodium has been adjusted upward from the USDA baseline to reflect typical spice-marinated restaurant preparation. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — roasted lamb ribs contain no digestible carbohydrate; this is naturally zero for a pure meat dish with dry spice rub containing negligible starch. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero for this preparation. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a meat-only dish with no plant-based components contributing fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — lamb meat contains no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for roasted lamb rib. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this specific cut and preparation. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this specific cut and preparation. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this dish contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' per convention for zero-carbohydrate meat dishes, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification principles and Veritas v2 scoring model requirements for GI > 0. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate content per serving is 0 g. 4. Sodium value of 380 mg/100 g is an upward adaptation from the USDA baseline (~75 mg/100 g for unseasoned roasted lamb rib) to reflect typical Chinese restaurant spice-marinated preparation with salt, soy-based marinades, and cumin-chili rub. 5. Halal status is marked true on the assumption that Bei Jiang Restaurant uses halal-certified lamb; this should be verified with the restaurant directly. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Noodle' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform categorisation error and has been disregarded; this dish is a meat dish, not a noodle dish.
